citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
One female Bichon "Gwen" MC# 5219 was identified by inspectors to be limping and not weight bearing on her rear left
leg Upon examination, there were two dried, reddened soft tissue wounds approximately the size of dime each on the toe
paw pads of her rear left leg. The dog did pull back when palpated on this foot. Additionally, this dog has long nails
present. Untreated lameness is causing pain and possible distress to this animal. The facility must use appropriate
methods to prevent, control, diagnose, and treat diseases and injuries in the regulated animals. The facility must ensure
each animal receives adequate veterinary care at all times. The facility must have this dog evaluated by a licensed
veterinarian to assess their health to develop an effective treatment plan. These medical records must be available for
APHIS inspection.

Two enclosures with 4 adult dogs had pitting, rusted wire on the front of their outdoor portion of their enclosures.
Excessive rust may prevent proper cleaning and sanitizing and lead to exposure of disease hazards. The surfaces of
housing facilities - including houses, dens, and other furniture-type fixtures and objects within the facility - must be
constructed in a manner and made of materials that allow them to be readily cleaned and sanitized, or removed or
replaced when worn or soiled. Interior surfaces and any surfaces that come in contact with dogs must be free of
excessive rust that prevents the required cleaning and sanitization, or that affects the structural strength of the surface.
To be corrected by: 20-April-2026

There were 3 enclosures each housing one puppy (under 4 months old) and one young adult (dog over 4 months old) that
was not the puppy's dam or foster dam. Housing puppies with adult dogs that are not their dam or foster dam may result
in injuries due to compatibility problems. Puppies under 4 months of age or less may not be housed in the same primary
enclosure with adult dogs other than their dams or foster dams.
This was corrected at the time of inspection.
To be corrected by: 15-April-2026

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The facility has not been sanitizing as often as necessary to prevent an accumulation of organic debris in primary
enclosures. In at least 2 primary enclosures housing 4 dogs, there is a light film of cobwebs, hair and dry organic material
on the top of the wire enclosures. One enclosure housing 2 adult dogs had a wet film of hair and organic material on the
front of the outdoor portion of the wire enclosure. Failure to sanitize enclosures often enough to prevent an accumulation
of debris may increase the risk of disease hazards towards the dogs.
Used primary enclosures and food and water receptacles for dogs must be sanitized at least once every 2 weeks and
more often if necessary to prevent an accumulation of dirt, debris, food waste, excreta, and other disease hazards. Hard
surfaces must be sanitized using one of the following methods: live steam under pressure or washing with hot water (at
least 180° F) and soap or detergent. The licensee must clean and sanitize the primary enclosures and ensure that all
aspects of the enclosure are maintained in a clean and sanitized condition in order to support the health of the dogs.

The attending veterinarian did not complete the regularly scheduled site visit within 12 months of each visit. One was
completed on May 25, 2024 and the next was completed on September 17, 2025. Regularly scheduled site visits are
essential in determining if all aspects of animal care (nutrition, health, environment, water, and air quality, etc.) are being
provided to the dogs. The licensee must have regularly scheduled visits, not less than once every 12 months, by the
attending veterinarian to the facility where animals are kept, to assess and ensure the adequacy of veterinary care and
other aspects of animal care and use.
To be corrected by: 15-April-2026

The complete physical examinations from head to tail on approximately 78 adult dogs by the attending veterinarian was
not completed within 12 months. One was completed in May 25, 2024 and then the next was performed September 17,
2025. Failure to have each dog examined at least once every 12 months may result in delay of treatment and diagnosis
for disease, illness, or injury. Correct by ensuring that a complete physical examination from head to tail of each adult dog
by the attending veterinarian is conducted not less than once every 12 months.
To be corrected by: 15-April-2026

The facility is not following their preventative plan for properly trimmed nails. The plan states adult dog's nails will be
trimmed once monthly, seven adult dogs were identified with long nails and the licensee stated it had likely been more
than a month since they were last trimmed. Long nails may affect the gait of the animal and lead to possible injuries. The
facility must follow their program of veterinary care's preventative care and treatment plans for healthy and unmatted hair
coats, properly trimmed nails, and clean and healthy eyes, ears, skin, and teeth,
To be corrected by: 20-April-2026

The facility is not recording the medication name and date of the sedation reversal medication given during procedures.
Without proper documentation, it is difficult to determine if the animals received adequate veterinary care. Dealers must
keep copies of medical records for dogs and make the records available for APHIS inspection. These records must
include the names of all vaccines and treatments administered and the dates of administration.
To be corrected: 15-April-2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
Additional Inspectors:
Jaclyn Melvin, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.40(b)(2)  Direct Repeat
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
One female Bichon "Gwen" MC# 5219 was identified by inspectors to be limping and not weight bearing on her rear left 
leg Upon examination, there were two dried, reddened soft tissue wounds approximately the size of dime each on the toe

veterinarian to assess their health to develop an effective treatment plan. These medical records must be available for 
APHIS inspection. 
3.1(c)(1)(i) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
Two enclosures with 4 adult dogs had pitting, rusted wire on the front of their outdoor portion of their enclosures. 
Excessive rust may prevent proper cleaning and sanitizing and lead to exposure of disease hazards. The surfaces of

replaced when worn or soiled. Interior surfaces and any surfaces that come in contact with dogs must be free of 
excessive rust that prevents the required cleaning and sanitization, or that affects the structural strength of the surface. 
To be corrected by: 20-April-2026 
3.7(c)

There were 3 enclosures each housing one puppy (under 4 months old) and one young adult (dog over 4 months old) that 
was not the puppy's dam or foster dam. Housing puppies with adult dogs that are not their dam or foster dam may result 
in injuries due to compatibility problems. Puppies under 4 months of age or less may not be housed in the same primary 
enclosure with adult dogs other than their dams or foster dams. 
This was corrected at the time of inspection.

3.11(b)(2)  Repeat

Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The facility has not been sanitizing as often as necessary to prevent an accumulation of organic debris in primary

Used primary enclosures and food and water receptacles for dogs must be sanitized at least once every 2 weeks and

3.13(a)(1)

The attending veterinarian did not complete the regularly scheduled site visit within 12 months of each visit. One was

The complete physical examinations from head to tail on approximately 78 adult dogs by the attending veterinarian was

Veterinary care for dogs.

To be corrected: 15-April-2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
